Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Alexander Bain

1818 – 1903

The psycho-physiologist behind British associationism and the founder of Mind.

Alexander Bain’s psychology can be reconstructed as a systematic philosophy of mind only if we avoid reading him as merely a Victorian “associationist” in the narrow textbook sense. He is that, but he is more than that. His mature position combines empirical psychology, physiological naturalism, anti-Cartesian mind-body theory, a motor theory of consciousness and volition, an associationist account of intellect, a developmental account of the self, and a social-naturalistic account of conscience. The resulting view is best described as a psychophysical, dual-aspect naturalism: mind and body are not two independent interacting substances, but two inseparable sides of one sentient organismic reality. Mind is not reduced away into matter, yet it is never scientifically available as a disembodied soul.

Bain’s distinctive philosophical importance lies in the transition he represents. He stands between the older British and Scottish philosophy of mind, still shaped by faculty psychology, common-sense metaphysics, introspection, and the “soul,” and the later scientific psychology of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. His work helps move philosophy of mind from the question “what immaterial substance thinks?” toward the question “what are the empirical, developmental, bodily, neural, affective, and associative conditions of mental life?”

1. Historical position and intellectual setting

Alexander Bain was born in Aberdeen in 1818 and died there in 1903. He was a Scottish philosopher and psychologist, taught logic and English at the University of Aberdeen, was closely associated with John Stuart Mill, and became one of the principal nineteenth-century advocates of a rigorous, scientific psychology grounded in the brain and nervous system. Britannica summarizes his significance by saying that he “advanced psychology” and sought physical correlatives for “idea” and “mind,” stressing the brain and nervous system.

His main works for philosophy of mind are The Senses and the Intellect first published in 1855, The Emotions and the Will in 1859, and Mind and Body: The Theories of Their Relation in 1873. Bain also founded the journal Mind in 1876, with George Croom Robertson as its first editor. The journal was explicitly created amid the growing professionalization of English-language psychology and philosophy, and its early editorial problem was to determine the scientific standing of psychology.

That institutional fact matters. Bain is not merely offering another speculative theory of the soul. He is participating in the creation of psychology as an empirical discipline while still writing within philosophy. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy places him within Scottish philosophy but stresses his anti-metaphysical tendency: he gave priority to psychology over metaphysics and pushed the study of mind in an empirical direction.

This explains the apparent dual character of Bain’s work. On one side, he is still a philosopher of mind in the classical sense: he discusses consciousness, self, belief, will, free will, moral obligation, and mind-body relations. On the other side, he treats these topics with the methods of natural history, physiology, developmental observation, pathology, comparative animal psychology, and introspection. His work is therefore a bridge between associationist philosophy and scientific psychology.

2. Bain’s method: psychology separated from metaphysics, but not from philosophy

Bain’s psychology begins from a deliberate methodological reform. In The Senses and the Intellect, he says that his purpose is to give a systematic account of the first two divisions of the science of mind, namely the senses and the intellect, with the remaining divisions, emotions and will, treated separately. He also insists that discoveries concerning the physiology of the nervous system must find their place in the science of mind.

This already marks a departure from purely introspective or faculty-based philosophy. Bain does not deny introspection; in fact, he thinks the mental world is known immediately only through self-consciousness. But he does not think introspection alone is enough. The mind must be studied through its bodily accompaniments, its development, its expression, its breakdowns, and its overt action.

He is explicit that psychology should be freed from excessive metaphysical entanglement. In the later edition of The Senses and the Intellect, he says systematic psychology should be “disburdened” of metaphysical and epistemological questions. That does not mean that Bain has no metaphysics. Rather, it means that metaphysical questions about the ultimate existence of the external world, the final nature of substance, or theological theories of the soul should not control the empirical science of mind.

This methodological separation appears especially clearly in his treatment of perception. Bain distinguishes the metaphysical question of whether the external world exists independently of the perceiving mind from the psychological question of how the notion of externality is generated. Psychology, for him, studies the genesis of the mind’s belief in externality; metaphysics may ask whether that belief corresponds to an independently existing world.

This distinction is one of Bain’s most important contributions. He does not simply deny metaphysics; he relocates it. Psychology comes first because it tells us what the mind actually does, how its states arise, how they are embodied, and how beliefs and selves are formed. Philosophy of mind must therefore be continuous with empirical psychology.

3. The basic definition of mind: subjectivity, feeling, volition, thought

Bain defines mind by contrast with the external or object world. The object world is characterized by extension. The inner or subject world consists of experiences that are not extended: pleasures, pains, volitions, thoughts, sensations, memories, and ideas.

This is not a naïve materialism. Bain does not say that mental facts are secretly extended objects. The contrast between mind and matter is basic. The body is known as an extended object; the mind is known as unextended subjectivity. He says mental phenomena fall under three ultimate heads: Feeling, Volition or Will, and Thought or Intellect.

These three divisions are not separate “faculties” in the old scholastic sense. Bain abandons faculty psychology as a primary explanatory scheme and instead organizes the mind around laws of association, bodily conditions, and elementary psychological functions.

The threefold classification is therefore analytic, not ontological. Feeling, will, and thought are distinguishable aspects of mental life, but actual mental states usually contain more than one aspect. Sensation, for example, is partly feeling and partly cognition. A perception may have emotional tone, discriminative structure, and volitional relevance all at once. Bain expressly says that the same mental state may be both intellectual and emotional, depending on whether it contains discrimination, comparison, retentiveness, pleasure, or pain.

The most fundamental mark of mind, however, is feeling. Bain says feeling is the foremost and most unmistakable sign of mind, and he notes that direct evidence of feeling is available only in one’s own consciousness; the feelings of other persons and animals must be inferred from outward signs.

This already yields a compact Bainian thesis: mind is not first a rational faculty, nor first a pure knowing subject, nor first a disembodied ego. Mind is first sentient, affective, active, and embodied.

4. Mind and body: neither Cartesian dualism nor reductive materialism

Bain’s most mature philosophy of mind appears in Mind and Body. There he frames the central issue as the relation between mind and brain. He considers several possible answers, including the view that mind and body are independent, but his own conclusion is that their connection is not occasional or partial; it is complete and pervasive.

His doctrine is often described as psychophysical parallelism, but that label is not quite sufficient. Bain does not merely say that mental and bodily events run in parallel. He says that the proper mental fact is a two-sided fact: a subjective mental process together with its physical basis. Conversely, a merely physical fact is one-sided, objective, and extended.

He repeatedly emphasizes that every mental process is accompanied by bodily process. In The Senses and the Intellect, he calls the thorough-going concomitance of physical processes with mental processes a law of all mind. In Mind and Body, he says that bodily changes affect mental states and mental changes bodily states; feelings and moods vary with hunger, fatigue, rest, air, temperature, drugs, injury, disease, sleep, age, and other bodily conditions.

Bain also appeals to brain pathology. Brain derangement has corresponding mental symptoms; mental life is at the mercy of bodily condition; and the physician assumes that every mental change has a corresponding physical change. He argues that thinking, sensation, and volition take time because nerve-force takes time: mental decision is subject to measurable temporal conditions and cannot transcend the pace of the bodily process.

Yet Bain refuses a crude reduction of mind to matter. He says matter and mind are two ultimate kinds or aspects and are not resolved into a higher common term. The most accurate reconstruction of his view is therefore this:

Bain accepts an irreducible contrast between subjective, unextended mind and objective, extended matter, while denying that the two are independent substances. Mental life is real, but it is never found apart from bodily life. Body and mind are distinguishable only as two aspects of one psychophysical process.

Bain himself moves toward the language of a single substance with two sets of properties. He says that the old arguments for two substances have lost validity and that the growing view is a single substance with physical and mental sides, a “double-faced unity.”

This places him near what later philosophers would call dual-aspect monism or a form of property dualism without substance dualism. He is not an eliminative materialist because he does not erase the subjective side. He is not a Cartesian dualist because he rejects independent mental substance. He is not an idealist because he gives the bodily and neural organism indispensable explanatory standing. His view is best called psychophysical naturalism.

5. Bain on the soul and incorporeal mind

Bain’s position on the soul is historically important because he does not merely dismiss the soul as a superstition; he gives a historical and psychological account of why soul-theories arose and why they became philosophically obsolete.

In Mind and Body, he classifies soul theories under doctrines of two substances and doctrines of one substance. He treats the history of immaterialism as a development and decay: the soul is variously said to partake of deity, to lack a determinate place, to be compatible with reason and thought in a way matter is not, to possess dignity, indivisibility, pure substantiality, active force, life-giving power, and personal identity.

He also traces early soul-language to bodily and quasi-material images: shadow, heart, blood, breath. Greek terms such as psyche, pneuma, and Latin spiritus derive from breath-like conceptions. Bain argues that early stages of thought do not yet possess a refined conception of an immaterial soul; such immaterialism becomes available only later, especially through Greek philosophy.

His criticism of incorporeal mind is direct. He says we have no direct experience or knowledge of mind apart from body. He also rejects the idea that the physical chain of nervous events is interrupted by an immaterial substance. There is no “rupture” in nervous continuity where a disembodied mental agent steps in.

Bain’s alternative is not that the soul is replaced by mere matter, but that the explanatory unit is the embodied psychophysical being. Mental and physical processes proceed together as “undivided twins”; the evidence supports two-sided causation, not a one-sided immaterial cause acting on a passive body.

This has major consequences for his philosophy of mind. The self is not a simple soul-substance. Volition is not the act of an incorporeal power. Memory is not a spiritual faculty independent of neural habit. Emotion is not an inner ghost-state; it has bodily expression and diffusion. Conscience is not an innate divine spark; it develops socially. Bain’s psychology therefore naturalizes the domains traditionally assigned to the soul.

His view should not be overstated as a dogmatic proof that no disembodied mind could exist in any metaphysical sense. His actual claim is more disciplined: psychology and physiology give us no scientific knowledge of mind apart from body, and every known mental fact belongs to a psychophysical sequence. The incorporeal soul therefore has no explanatory function in the science of mind.

6. Feeling: the affective foundation of mind

Bain gives feeling priority. This does not mean that all mental states are pleasures or pains, because he allows neutral states and intellectual states. But feeling is the clearest mark of consciousness and the point from which volition, action, and valuation emerge.

In The Emotions and the Will, Bain treats emotions not as disembodied inner episodes but as states with bodily expression, conscious tone, pleasure or pain, excitement, and practical consequences. His classification of emotions depends not merely on introspective quality but also on bodily diffusion and resulting conduct.

In Mind and Body, he formulates a law of self-conservation: pleasure is connected with the increase or furtherance of vital functions, and pain with their abatement or obstruction. This physiological interpretation is essential. Feeling is not a private mental vapor floating above the organism. It is the subjective side of bodily vitality, stimulation, diffusion, and organic condition.

Bain also emphasizes expression. The bodily accompaniments of emotion are not incidental signs; they are part of the natural history of emotion. Gestures and expressions become a kind of universal language, and Bain criticizes spiritualists and metaphysicians for overlooking this bodily manifestation of feeling.

The philosophical result is an embodied affectivism. Feeling is not a lower faculty beneath reason; it is the basal condition of mindedness. It supplies the motive force for action, the tone of experience, the materials of emotion, and much of the evidence by which we infer the minds of others.

7. Volition: spontaneous movement, pleasure-pain, and acquired control

Bain’s theory of will is one of his most original contributions. Against theories that begin with deliberate choice, he begins with movement. In The Senses and the Intellect, he argues that muscular feelings are distinct from the special senses and that movement and the feeling of movement precede ordinary sense experience. He says active energy from internal impulses, independent of external impressions, is a primary fact.

This is a major reversal of passive empiricism. Bain does not picture the mind as first receiving sensations and only later acting. The organism is spontaneously active before it is intelligently voluntary. Movements arise from internal bodily and nervous energy; through pleasure, pain, repetition, and association, some of these movements become purposive acts.

He insists that movements are anterior to and independent of the special senses and that nerve centers can prompt action without external sensory stimulation. Spontaneous activity is therefore the essential prelude to voluntary power.

The transition from spontaneity to will occurs through pleasure and pain. Bain’s general law of volition is that pleasure prompts action tending toward its continuance, increase, or renewal, while pain prompts action tending toward its cessation, abatement, or prevention.

The primitive organism moves spontaneously. Some movements increase pleasure or reduce pain; those movements are retained, repeated, and associated with the relevant feeling. Others fail or worsen the state and are dropped. Bain describes a trial-and-error process: painful movements are checked, favorable movements are retained, and the movement becomes connected with the present feeling.

This is a proto-learning theory of action. It anticipates, without yet possessing the later experimental apparatus, ideas that would become important in behaviorism and reinforcement learning: spontaneous action, selection by consequence, habit formation, and the gradual acquisition of voluntary control. But Bain’s account remains mentalistic: pleasure, pain, feeling, and consciousness are still central.

He also rejects the idea that desire is a primitive unanalyzable faculty. Desire is not basic; its antecedents are pleasure, pain, or a fixed idea. The will is not an original sovereign power. It is built out of spontaneous motor activity, affective valuation, association, and repetition.

This theory deeply affects Bain’s treatment of free will. In The Emotions and the Will, he argues that the “liberty and necessity” problem is made mysterious by unsuitable language. If sensation, emotion, intellect, and other parts of mind can be scientifically explained, will is not peculiarly insoluble. He treats conflict of motives as a natural psychological process and says the ultimate facts are retreat from pain and pursuit of pleasure.

So Bain is a naturalistic compatibilist or motivational determinist, depending on terminology. He does not deny deliberation, resolution, effort, or self-control; indeed, he studies them closely. But he denies that they require an uncaused spiritual faculty. Volition is lawful, embodied, affectively driven, and educable.

8. Intellect: discrimination, similarity, retentiveness, and association

Bain’s theory of intellect is associationist, but not simplistic. He does not merely say that ideas mechanically follow one another. He identifies three fundamental intellectual functions: discrimination, similarity, and retentiveness.

Discrimination is consciousness of difference. Without difference, there is no cognition. Similarity is consciousness of agreement and allows generalization, classification, recognition, analogy, and reasoning. Retentiveness is the mind’s capacity to preserve and revive previous states; it underlies memory, habit, acquisition, and learning.

The law of contiguity is central to retentiveness. Actions, sensations, and feelings that occur together or in close succession cohere and later revive one another. This is the basis of memory, habit, and acquired powers. Bain explicitly links this psychological process to brain plasticity: the acquisition of names, objects, movements, and feelings involves the same plastic operation of the brain.

His associationism also includes compound association, where multiple links cooperate. Bain does not think this requires a new mental principle; emotion and volition may help or hinder intellectual forces, but the explanatory machinery remains associative.

Higher cognition is constructed from these simpler functions. Memory is literal reproduction; conception and imagination involve constructive picturing; scientific abstraction, induction, and deduction primarily depend on similarity, with constructive processes added.

The philosophical upshot is that Bain rejects a faculty psychology in which reason, judgment, imagination, memory, and understanding are distinct powers of a rational soul. He decomposes them into more elementary operations: difference, agreement, retention, association, construction, and motor-affective support.

This makes his account both empiricist and developmental. The intellect is not given as a complete rational structure. It is built through experience, association, habit, language, bodily action, and social training.

9. Perception, space, externality, and the muscular sense

Bain’s theory of perception is one of the most philosophically interesting parts of his system. He gives the muscular sense and bodily movement a foundational role in the perception of external reality, space, extension, and solidity.

He argues that solidity is a complex involving touch and muscularity, while space is understood through movement; resistance and empty space are correlatives. Vision, space, and form require movement, and the conjoint experience of senses and movements furnishes the notion of extended matter.

He also says that pure passive sensation would not yield our perception of an external world. Movement gives sensation a new character: the sense of force, resistance, direction, and range. The notion of externality is generated by the active organism encountering constraint and possibility in movement.

This makes Bain an early theorist of active perception. The world is not first a picture projected into an inner theater. The object-world is constituted for the subject through sensorimotor experience: movement, touch, resistance, muscular effort, and coordinated sensation. In this respect Bain anticipates later themes in pragmatism, sensorimotor theories of perception, ecological psychology, and embodied cognition.

But he does not collapse the external world into mere sensation. His point is psychological, not metaphysical. He is explaining how the mind comes to possess the notion of externality, not claiming to have settled the ultimate metaphysical status of matter.

10. Consciousness: not merely inwardness

Bain criticizes narrow uses of “consciousness” that identify it only with passive inner awareness. He distinguishes conscious states from latent mental trains and habitual mechanical actions; acquired habits can become nearly unconscious.

He also argues that consciousness should not be confined to inward passivity. Active energy and outward work belong to mental life. This is important because much philosophy had treated consciousness as contemplation, awareness, or representation. Bain makes it active, embodied, motoric, and practical.

He distinguishes emotional and intellectual consciousness. Emotional consciousness involves feeling, pleasure, pain, and excitement. Intellectual consciousness involves discrimination, especially consciousness of difference. Bain even allows for neutral intellectual excitement that is neither pleasure nor pain.

This yields a layered account of consciousness. At the base is sentience or feeling. Alongside it is discriminative awareness. Above that are associated perception, memory, judgment, imagination, belief, and reflective self-consciousness. Consciousness is therefore not a single simple light but a complex set of affective, cognitive, and volitional modes.

11. Belief and knowledge: the active criterion of reality

Bain’s account of belief is strikingly action-centered. He argues that belief has no meaning except in relation to action; no mere conception, without practical bearing, amounts to belief.

Belief is thus not simply an inner assent to a proposition. It is expectation, readiness, confidence, and orientation toward possible action. Bain says belief concerns the contingent future following action, and action is the ultimate criterion of belief.

This view is proto-pragmatist. To believe is to be disposed to act, rely, expect, and organize conduct. Knowledge is not passive representation; it is bound to attention, selection, action, and confidence. Bain says sensation alone is not knowledge; discriminative consciousness is required, and higher perception and cognition require associated impressions plus belief.

He is also aware of the non-rational sources of belief. Experience and trial are the basis of belief, but instincts and emotions also determine convictions and may oppose experience, producing fallacy or delusion. Testimony likewise contains evidence and feeling: the emotional influence of a speaker or multitude can shape belief.

This is a sophisticated psychology of belief. Bain treats belief as an embodied, affective, social, and practical phenomenon. It is not reducible to logic. Evidence matters, but conviction is also shaped by habit, emotion, authority, repetition, and action.

12. The self and personal identity

Bain’s self is not a Cartesian ego and not a simple soul. It is a developmental construction built from bodily sensations, active effort, memory, language, social relations, and interests.

In The Senses and the Intellect, he treats the self, ego, and personality as topics of genesis. He allows that the primitive distinction between self and not-self may involve inherited preparation, but the actual development of self-consciousness must be explained psychologically.

The infant begins with organic sensations and bodily experiences. The child cognizes its own body and becomes aware of agency through elementary efforts to avoid pain and attain gratification. Later, ideation separates from immediate sensation; language, especially personal pronouns, and social relations make the child aware of itself as a bodily and mental entity among other persons.

The inner self, distinguished from the bodily self, develops later through education and reflection. Personal identity is supported by retrospective memory organized in chronological order. Bain says the combined bodily and mental self is easier to conceive than a purely mental self, and only special occasions lead us to distinguish mind from body sharply.

He finally analyzes personality as the grand total of one’s interests, composed of sensations, emotions, volitions, and ideas. He separates this psychological account from theological or ontological questions.

This is a major anti-substantialist account of the self. The self is not an inner metaphysical owner of experiences. It is the organized unity of bodily life, memory, agency, affect, interest, social recognition, and reflective thought.

13. Moral psychology and conscience

Bain’s account of morality follows the same naturalistic pattern. He rejects the idea of an innate, simple, indivisible moral sense. In The Emotions and the Will, he says the moral sense is not one and indivisible; right and wrong require a paramount volitional force, and when that force is absent society supplies sanctions and punishments.

He observes that moral rules vary widely among societies, but obligatory observances share a common feature: imposition backed by sanction. He compares morality with public legislation and treats punishment as both an instrument and criterion of the obligatory.

Conscience, for Bain, develops through social obedience, education, discipline, and habit. Over time, imposed obligations may become internal and self-imposed. A general feeling of duty can survive even after particular rules are questioned or changed. But this does not prove an original innate faculty of abstract duty; Bain explicitly says abstract morality is not original or innate.

He further distinguishes forms of conscience, including slavish, citizen, and independent conscience, and argues that genuinely self-originating conscience is rare. This rarity shows, in his view, how little conscience is simply innate.

Philosophically, this places morality within the same system as the rest of mind. Moral life is affective, volitional, associative, social, and developmental. The moral self is trained, not implanted ready-made. Bain’s account anticipates later social and psychological theories of moral internalization, although his reliance on punishment and sanction gives his theory a strongly disciplinary cast.

14. Free will, determinism, and agency

Bain’s theory of will leaves little room for libertarian free will in the traditional metaphysical sense. He does not treat choice as an uncaused act of a spiritual agent. He treats it as the outcome of motive conflict, affective pressure, acquired habits, deliberation, and associative control.

However, he is not denying agency. On the contrary, agency is central to his psychology. He simply relocates agency from a metaphysical soul to the embodied organism. The agent acts because feelings, ideas, habits, and motives organize movement.

The traditional language of liberty and necessity is, for Bain, misleading. The will is not uniquely mysterious. It is part of the same lawful mental-natural order as sensation, emotion, and thought.

This view has two sides. Negatively, Bain rejects the soul-based conception of free will. Positively, he gives a psychology of self-control. Deliberation, effort, moral habit, duty, and resolution are real, but they are built through education and experience. A person becomes free in the practical sense not by escaping causality, but by acquiring stable motives, habits, and powers of self-regulation.

15. Reconstructing Bain’s complete philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Bainian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, mind is the subjective side of experience. It is known by feeling, volition, and thought, and is contrasted with the extended object-world. The mind is not itself an extended thing.

Second, mind and body are inseparable in fact and explanation. Every mental event has a bodily concomitant; the brain is the chief organ of mind; mental life is never scientifically encountered apart from the organism.

Third, the best ontology is dual-aspect naturalism. Mind and matter are irreducibly contrasted, but they are not independent substances. The same being is knowable from the subjective and objective sides. Bain’s “double-faced unity” is the mature metaphysical core of his system.

Fourth, the incorporeal soul has no scientific explanatory role. Bain does not derive psychology from theology or spiritual substance. He treats soul theories historically and argues that we have no direct knowledge of mind apart from body.

Fifth, feeling is the primary sign of mind. The mind is first sentient and affective. Pleasure, pain, emotion, organic condition, and bodily expression are central to mental life.

Sixth, movement precedes fully formed cognition and will. Bain gives spontaneous activity and muscular sense a foundational role. The organism is active before it is reflectively voluntary.

Seventh, volition is organized by pleasure and pain. Actions that maintain or renew pleasure and remove or prevent pain become selected, retained, and associated.

Eighth, intellect is built from discrimination, similarity, and retentiveness. Reason, memory, imagination, judgment, and abstraction are not separate faculties of a soul but complex developments of elementary psychological functions.

Ninth, perception is active and sensorimotor. Space, solidity, and externality arise through the coordination of sensation with muscular movement, effort, resistance, and bodily activity.

Tenth, belief is practical. To believe is not merely to entertain an idea but to be disposed to act, expect, rely, and organize conduct. Belief is shaped by experience, but also by emotion, instinct, testimony, and social influence.

Eleventh, the self is developmental and composite. Personal identity is not a simple substance but the organization of bodily awareness, memory, agency, language, social recognition, and interests.

Twelfth, morality is socially internalized volition. Conscience is not an innate indivisible faculty but a product of sanction, education, habit, sympathy, resentment, self-command, and social discipline.

Together these theses form a coherent system. Bain’s philosophy of mind is a theory of the embodied, active, affective, associative, socially trained organism.

16. Bain’s place in the larger development of philosophy of mind

Bain’s importance is easiest to see by locating him between older and newer paradigms.

Against Cartesian substance dualism, he denies that psychology should posit an independent immaterial mind interacting with the body. There is no experiential or physiological evidence for mind apart from body, and no gap in the nervous sequence where an incorporeal agent must enter.

Against faculty psychology, he rejects the view that memory, reason, imagination, conscience, and will are primitive powers of the soul. He explains them through simpler processes: association, discrimination, similarity, retentiveness, affect, movement, habit, and social training.

Against passive empiricism, he insists on spontaneous movement, muscular sense, action, and bodily activity. This is one of the most forward-looking aspects of his work. The mind is not merely impressed upon by sensations; it explores, moves, resists, tries, fails, repeats, and learns.

Against pure introspectionism, he brings in physiology, expression, pathology, animal behavior, infant development, and action. But against reductive materialism, he preserves the irreducible subjective character of feeling, thought, and will.

Historically, Bain’s associationism belongs to a line running from Hume and Hartley through James Mill and John Stuart Mill. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Bain as a sophisticated empiricist associationist, noting his distinctive attention to instincts, reflexes, spontaneous action, neuroanatomy, nervous energy, and the contact between physiology and psychology.

The Stanford Encyclopedia’s discussion of associationism emphasizes that associationist theories connect learning and thought through causal history, treating experience as shaping cognitive architecture; this tradition runs through British empiricism and later behaviorist approaches. Bain belongs centrally to this tradition, but his version is unusually embodied and motoric.

His founding role in Mind also signals his transitional position. The journal’s early concern with the scientific standing of psychology, and its publication of landmark work by figures such as Darwin and William James, places Bain at the threshold of modern psychology and analytic philosophy of mind.

In retrospect, Bain anticipates several later developments:

He anticipates psychophysical psychology by insisting on lawful correlations between mental and neural processes.

He anticipates behaviorist themes by treating action, habit, consequence, pleasure, pain, and observable expression as central to psychology.

He anticipates pragmatist theories of belief by defining belief through action, expectation, and practical reliance.

He anticipates embodied cognition by making movement, muscular sense, and bodily activity foundational to perception and volition.

He anticipates developmental psychology by explaining selfhood, conscience, and volition through growth, learning, and education.

He anticipates social psychology of morality by treating conscience as internalized sanction and socially formed obligation.

These are anticipations, not identities. Bain’s physiology is nineteenth-century physiology; his associationism lacks the resources of modern cognitive science; his hedonic theory of will is too simple for the full complexity of motivation; and his account of morality can seem overly punitive or sanction-centered. But his philosophical architecture remains important because it systematically replaces the immaterial soul with an embodied, affective, active, associative mind.

17. Final assessment

Bain’s philosophy of mind is a rigorous naturalistic alternative to both Cartesian dualism and reductive materialism. Its central claim is that mind is real as subjective experience, but exists and operates only as the mental side of an embodied organism. There is no need for a separable soul to explain sensation, thought, will, selfhood, belief, or morality.

His psychology begins with feeling, not abstract reason; with movement, not passive sensation; with bodily expression, not hidden spiritual essence; with association and habit, not innate faculties; with development, not ready-made selfhood; and with social training, not an original moral sense.

The most compact formula for Bain’s philosophy of mind is this:

Mind is the unextended, subjective, affective-intellectual-volitional side of an embodied nervous organism; its operations are lawful, developmental, associative, motoric, and socially shaped; and the hypothesis of an incorporeal soul is historically intelligible but scientifically unnecessary.

That is Bain’s enduring place in the history of philosophy of mind: he helps transform the “mind” from the activity of a metaphysical soul into the organized life of a sentient, acting, remembering, believing, self-forming body.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
